text: As a director, do you regret not using
AI to possibly be more quick and
efficient Fader 2? Good question.
No, I do not regret not using AI for
Vader episode 2 simply because, and I've
said this many times, I'll just make it
clear again. AI is fun to watch for
these little videos on YouTube. But you
don't get me wrong, they have a lot of
issues. Like they do not evoke emotion,
right? They they do feel somewhat hollow
no matter how good. And that's simply
because AI is not at the point yet where
it can evoke every single little nuance
of facial expression that you may not
even notice or be able to register in
your brain, but you subconsciously do.
Or how a person walks or, you know,
there will be things that do look
robotic or like a machine created it.
And so at the very heart of it, yeah,
it's very entertaining. It's very cool.
It's very fun. But at the moment, it
will not be as true to form as actual
animation or live action. So the cost
effectiveness of AI is definitely there.
It's definitely prevalent and lucrative
if you want to think of it like that.
And it saves you a lot of money. And it
would have saved me a ton of money,
hundreds and hundreds of thousands of
dollars and headaches and time. But I do
believe in paying artists for a project
that I believe is going to be
revolutionary in the Star Wars community
and uh to be able to tell the story the
way that I want it to be told. I don't
think AI can do that. You're limited.
You're very limited with AI. It's like,
okay, so I can have this angle for, you
know, 4 seconds and then I got to switch
to another angle and no, I want it done
right. I got to pay the big bucks and
that's a burden on me, but the end
product will speak for itself and it's
going to be timeless. I don't think AI
will be able to replicate what Vader 2
will do within the next several years.
>> Well, if droids could think, there'd be
none of us here, would there?
>> So, therefore, I am not regretful of not
using AI. And we've employed a [ __ ] ton
of artists, man. Spent well over half a
million dollars already, all artists.
And in my career, I've definitely spent
over a million dollars on artists,
employing artists, paying artists,
respecting their work. So I I always
find it laughable when people are, you
know, saying, "Oh, he's endorsing AI
endorse real artists." It's like, I do.
And none of these people that are just
literal social justice warriors, uh, who
have probably never paid an artist in
their life and use chat GPT to do their
homework. I have no clue what they're
talking about, who they're talking to.
So, if anyone has a right to actually
observe AI or react to AI, it would be
me because I have paid and employed well
over a million dollars for artists all
around the world uh in my time. So, good
question. You know, on the on the
context of AI, to make it clear again,
yeah, they're really cool. They're fun
for these little projects and all that,
but you know, when it comes down to
making a real movie, making something
that will stand the test of time, making
something that will make you feel
something. Hire real artists. Make a
real project. make a real movie and it's
going to cost you money and it's going
to cost you time and stress and learning
curves because believe me, you're going
to dump money into making something and
you're going to direct it and you're
going to be like, "Well, this is kind of
shit." So, you have to perfect your
directing abilities and that just is
through failure and repetition really.
Um, and in being a director, I am
nowhere [ __ ] near where I'm going to
be in several years or, you know, where
I want to be. But that's trial and
error. And I'm sure I'll look back at
Vader 2 and be like, "God, that was god-
aful the way I I wanted things." But
that's where I am right now. And I'm
lucky to have a very talented team that
is in the industry, who knows how to
shoot films and direct and all that
stuff and gives me great pointers and
does a lot of great stuff themselves to
make the film synergistically, which is
what a movie is. A whole bunch of
different teams coming together to put
together something that's going to be
wellreceived. And that's why I love
movies is because you're using all of
the arts, you know, you using Yeah. VFX,
you're using artists, you're using
music, you're using sketch work. If it's
live action, you're using wardrobe.
There's so many different art
departments going into a movie to
synergistically come together and create
one beautiful piece that will speak to
your soul. And I don't think AI can do
that yet. I think VFX artists and I
think live action can do that. theory
probably regretting spending 10 mil on a
fan film when all these AI things are
probably going to look just as good,
maybe even better. Probably the most
incorrect comment I've seen all year.
There is no AI that could look better
than Vader episode 2 for probably the
next year at least. Not even on the same
playing field whatsoever.
The thing, you know, even beyond the
animation with Vader 2 compared to AI is
that I have obviously vision. I'm not
saying [ __ ] that sound bad.
>> It's insulting. I'm not saying guys who
do AI don't have vision, but the amount
of experience that I have with making
movies of like 10 years now on YouTube
and like even before that, you know,
being a kid making little home movies is
beyond anything that you can learn from
AI because AI doesn't really it does a
lot of the work for you. So, it's not
really like training your brain to
create something from scratch. So, what
I mean by that is also like the music,
the sound effects, which I was all
trained by when I was making
fanfictions, when I was making comic
videos. The little things that you
wouldn't even notice, but it like makes
it so much more impactful. That all
takes time, trial and error, constantly
watching something over and over again
to see, you know, what if I do this
here? What if I add that? What if I cut
this this way? That transition doesn't
work. What if I do this? This looks
better. No, I like it the other way.
That takes years of time, man. It's you
can't buy that. And a computer can't do
that unless you know what to look for.
So everything from the music to the
sound effects to [ __ ] like fading of
the sound effects when you fade it, when
you enter it, when you want it have that
crescendo, like when the beats, how
should the footsteps sound? These are
like little things that you would never
even think of. How should the sound of
the cape be? Should it be more crunchy?
Should it be more bassy? Like these
aren't things that you just put it into
AI and it's like, "Okay, yeah, it just
does a flag sound in the wind." You want
it to sound heavy. You can't do that
[ __ ] with AI. And even if you could, you
have to have the brain and the
experience and the directorial
experience, which comes from years of
failure. I look at Vader episode 1 now
and I'm like, "Wow, uh, there's a lot
that could have been done better, and I
should have directed that." That's a
whole another story.
You know how many times I had my SFX guy
go over this scene? This scene to do the
sound for this? I lost count so many
times because I was like, "Well, we need
it needs to be more crunchy, more heavy,
more punchy." cuz you're you're putting
in you're factoring in his weight plus
falling at a distance, whatever the
distance is, and gravity and how he
lands and the material that he's landing
on. How do you want that to translate
without having to read it or explain it
or anything and just from a [ __ ] 0.5
second sound effect? I imagine you you
mute this and you just have a squeak
sound. Do you see what I'm saying? It
sounds, it does not convey the level of
power or authority.
